sections.subtitle: Recent Form
sections.text: Everton have shown mixed results in their last 5 matches, with 1 win, 1 draw, and 3 losses, struggling to convert chances as seen in their low expected goals. Wolverhampton, however, have had a slightly better recent run with 1 win, 1 draw, and 3 losses, managing a strong 3-0 victory against West Ham. Their form suggests a slight edge in confidence coming into this match.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: In the last two encounters between these sides, results have been split, with Wolverhampton winning 2-0 in their most recent clash on September 23, 2025, while Everton took a 3-2 victory earlier on August 30, 2025. Matches between these teams tend to be competitive, often with both sides scoring, though clean sheets have eluded Everton recently against Wolves.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Everton, sitting 12th with 28 points, have a decent defensive record, conceding only 24 goals (6th best in the league), but their attack struggles with just 22 goals scored. Wolverhampton, languishing at 20th with only 6 points, have scored a mere 14 goals while conceding 40, highlighting their defensive frailties. Everton's superior league position and defensive stability give them a clear advantage.

sections.subtitle: Key Players and Absences
sections.text: Everton will rely on players like James Garner, who has been in form with a 7.72 average Sofascore rating over the last 5 matches, though they miss key midfielders like Kiernan Dewsbury-Hall. Wolverhampton lack standout performers in recent games and have several missing players, which could hinder their ability to compete effectively.

sections.subtitle: Match Outlook
sections.text: Given Everton's stronger league standing, home advantage at Hill Dickinson Stadium, and Wolves' poor away form, the Sofascore prediction of a 69% home win probability seems justified. Wolverhampton's defensive issues and lack of attacking threat make it difficult to see them overcoming Everton, who should dominate possession and create more chances.

halftimeSections.subtitle: First half overview
halftimeSections.text: The first half at Hill Dickinson Stadium saw Everton take a narrow lead over Wolverhampton with a 1-0 scoreline. Michael Keane's right-footed strike in the 17th minute proved to be the difference, capitalizing on one of Everton's created opportunities. Everton showed more clinical finishing compared to Wolverhampton, who struggled to convert their entries into the final third into meaningful chances. Both teams had fairly balanced possession, with Wolverhampton edging slightly ahead, but Everton made better use of their time on the ball in dangerous areas. Discipline-wise, Wolverhampton picked up a yellow card, while Everton maintained a clean slate.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Everton's attacking intent
halftimeSections.text: Everton displayed a stronger attacking intent in the first half, creating multiple big chances and getting shots on target. Their penetration into the penalty area was notable, and they showed dominance in duels, particularly in the air. Michael Keane was pivotal, not only scoring the opener but also contributing defensively. Players like James Garner and Tim Iroegbunam stood out with their creative contributions, setting up opportunities for their teammates. Everton's ability to win duels and maintain pressure in the final third suggests they have the upper hand going into the break.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Wolverhampton's struggles
halftimeSections.text: Wolverhampton, on the other hand, failed to register any significant threats in front of goal despite frequent entries into the final third. They lacked precision in their attacks, with no shots on target and no big chances created. Their duel success rate was considerably lower than Everton's, which hampered their ability to control the game physically. While they showed some resilience defensively with clearances and interceptions, their inability to turn possession into goal-scoring opportunities remains a concern. The yellow card picked up by José Sá also indicates some frustration in their ranks.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum shifts
halftimeSections.text: The first half saw momentum swinging between both sides at different stages. Everton started with early intent, pushing forward in the opening minutes before Wolverhampton took over with a sustained spell of pressure. However, Everton regained control around the time of their goal, launching a series of attacking moves that kept Wolverhampton on the back foot. Towards the latter part of the half, Everton maintained dominance with consistent forays into the opposition half, although Wolverhampton had brief moments of resurgence. Overall, Everton dictated the attacking tempo for the majority of the half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players to watch
halftimeSections.text: For Everton, Michael Keane will be crucial in the second half, having already made his mark with a goal and showing solidity at the back. James Garner’s creativity in midfield, with key passes and a high rating, could be the catalyst for further goals. On Wolverhampton's side, João Gomes has shown promise in midfield with tackles and interceptions, and he might be the key to unlocking Everton’s defense if given more attacking freedom. Hee-Chan Hwang also has the potential to turn things around if Wolverhampton can create clearer chances for him to exploit.
